questions.justification_correct: Under 18 USC 1956(a)(1)(B)(i), it is unlawful to conduct a financial transaction knowing that the property involved represents proceeds of specified unlawful activity, when the transaction is designed in whole or in part to conceal or disguise the nature, location, source, ownership, or control of the proceeds. This is the 'concealment' provision of the money laundering statute. The scenario describes classic concealment money laundering: the operator conducted wire transfers (financial transactions) knowing the funds were proceeds of securities fraud (a specified unlawful activity), and deliberately structured multiple transactions to different banks specifically to conceal the source and ownership of the fraudulent proceeds.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A (promotion money laundering under 1956(a)(1)(A)(i)) requires intent to promote the underlying crime, not present here where the fraud was complete. Option C (international transportation under 1956(a)(2)) requires specific intent to promote unlawful activity, while the facts indicate concealment intent. Option D refers to structuring to evade CTR requirements under 1956(a)(3), which is a separate offense from concealment money laundering; while there may be structuring, the primary conduct described is concealment of the proceeds' criminal origin through multiple layered transfers.

questions.text: A CFE has evidence that a defendant received $180,000 in cash proceeds from a drug trafficking organization and deposited the money into a legitimate business account at a national bank. The defendant used the account to pay business expenses including rent ($15,000), employee salaries ($45,000), and equipment purchases ($30,000). The defendant was not involved in the underlying drug trafficking. For purposes of 18 USC 1957 (engaging in monetary transactions in criminally derived property), which element distinguishes this statute from the concealment provision of 18 USC 1956?
options: A. 18 USC 1957 requires proof that the defendant knew the property was derived from drug trafficking specifically, whereas 18 USC 1956 requires only general knowledge of unlawful activity

B. 18 USC 1957 requires only that the defendant knew the property was derived from specified unlawful activity and engaged in a monetary transaction exceeding $10,000; it does not require proof of intent to conceal or promote
C. 18 USC 1957 applies only to cash transactions, while 18 USC 1956 applies to any financial transaction including wire transfers and check deposits
D. 18 USC 1957 requires proof that the monetary transaction was conducted through a financial institution, whereas 18 USC 1956 applies to any transaction affecting interstate commerce
questions.justification_correct: 18 USC 1957 prohibits engaging in a monetary transaction in criminally derived property of a value greater than $10,000, knowing that the property is derived from specified unlawful activity. The key distinction from 18 USC 1956(a)(1)(B) is that 1957 does NOT require proof of intent to conceal, disguise, or promote—it is a 'spending statute' that criminalizes knowing monetary transactions in criminal proceeds exceeding $10,000, regardless of purpose. This makes 1957 easier to prove than 1956 concealment charges because prosecutors need not establish specific intent beyond the knowledge that the property was criminally derived. In this scenario, the deposits and payments through a financial institution involving more than $10,000 in drug proceeds would violate 1957.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A is incorrect; both statutes require knowledge that property is derived from specified unlawful activity, not necessarily knowledge of the specific crime type. Option C is wrong; 1957 applies to 'monetary transactions' (broadly defined) not just cash, and 1956 similarly covers various transaction types. Option D is incorrect; while 1957 requires transactions through a financial institution or involving certain monetary instruments, this is not the primary distinguishing element from 1956's concealment provision—the key difference is the absence of a specific intent requirement in 1957.
